blocks.text: You already know two things: functions can return values, and if/else makes decisions. Now let's combine them.

A function that contains an if/else and returns a result is one of the most useful patterns in programming: it's exactly how Simon Says will check your answers. In the Simon Says game, the player clicks a sequence of colours.
The game needs to check: did the player get it right? That is a decision — and it happens inside a function called checkResult().
Here is the pattern — a function that makes a decision and returns the answer to the caller:

blocks.label: Simon Says connection
blocks.text: When you finish clicking, the game needs to know: did the player get it right? That question is answered by checkResult(). It compares two things and returns true or false — the caller decides what to do with that answer.

blocks.alt: checkResult() is a function that takes in two values, compares them, and returns true or false. The caller gets that answer back and can act on it.
blocks.caption: checkResult() is a function that takes in two values, compares them, and returns true or false. The caller gets that answer back and can act on it.

blocks.text: Here is an example of `checkResult()`. It takes two values, compares them, and returns true or false. The caller gets that answer back and can act on it.

blocks.label: return stops the function
blocks.text: The moment JavaScript hits a return statement, the function stops. Nothing after it runs. That is why you can have multiple return statements inside an if/else — only one of them will ever fire.

content.heading: In Simon-Says, checkResult() takes the action

blocks.text: The simplified version of `checkResult()` returns true or false.

The real game goes one step further — instead of returning an answer, `checkResult()` takes the action directly. If the player is correct, it increases the level and starts the next round. If wrong, it ends the game.

blocks.label: Two valid approaches
blocks.text: Both versions use if/else inside a function. The simplified one returns true or false and lets the caller decide what to do. The real one takes the action itself. Neither is wrong — they are just different ways to organise the same decision. You will explore this more in Session 5.

exercises.title: Write a decision function
tasks: Write a function called isCorrect(playerAnswer, correctAnswer) that returns true if both values match and false if they don't.
Call it four times: two matching pairs and two non-matching pairs.
Store each result in a variable and log it with a label.
Confirm you see two trues and two falses.
exercises.hint: Use === to compare. Return true inside the if block and false inside the else block.

exercises.title: Predict the output
tasks: Before running: write down what each console.log will print.
Run it and check your predictions.
For any wrong ones: trace through the function step by step and explain what happened.
exercises.hint: Follow the function top to bottom. Remember: the first return it hits stops the function.

exercises.title: Build a simplified checkResult()
tasks: Write a function called checkResult(playerAnswer, correctAnswer) that returns true if they match and false if not.
Write a second function called handleOutcome(result) that logs 'Correct! Well done.' if result is true, and 'Wrong. Try again.' if false.
Call checkResult and pass the return value straight into handleOutcome.
Test with a correct answer and an incorrect answer.
This is the pattern the Simon Says game uses to separate the decision from the action.
exercises.hint: You can pass a function call directly as an argument: handleOutcome(checkResult('blue', 'blue'))

checks.label: checkResult returns true for matching answers
checkResult returns false for non-matching answers
logs correct message for a match
logs wrong message for a non-match

challenges.title: The function always returns true
challenges.description: This function should return true when the score is 10 or higher and false otherwise. But it always returns true. Find the single bug.
challenges.hint: Read the condition carefully. score >= 0 is true for every possible score.
challenges.explanation: score >= 0 is true for any number including 5. The else branch never runs. Fix: change >= 0 to >= 10 so only scores of 10 and above return true.

challenges.title: The result is being ignored
challenges.description: checkResult returns true or false correctly, but the game never logs the outcome. The caller is not using the return value. Fix it so the correct message appears.
challenges.hint: The return value needs to be captured and used. Store it in a variable first, then use that variable in the if condition.

checks.label: logs Correct! for a matching answer

challenges.explanation: checkResult is called but its return value is thrown away. The if below it always checks true — a hardcoded value, not the result. Fix: let result = checkResult('blue', 'blue'); then if (result) { ... }.

challenges.title: The wrong branch fires for the right answer
challenges.description: The player typed the correct answer but the function returns false. There are two bugs. Find both.
challenges.hint: Look at both the comparison operator and the return values. One is the wrong type of equals, one is returning the wrong boolean.

checks.label: checkResult returns true for matching answers
checkResult returns false for non-matching answers

challenges.explanation: Two bugs: (1) == instead of === — always use triple equals. (2) The return values are swapped — the if branch (match) returns false and the else (no match) returns true. Fix both: change == to === and swap the return values.
